title: Amazon Product Catalog — System Design
description: Design the catalog behind a large e-commerce store: browse and search hundreds of millions of products, filter by facets, and render fast product pages — while inventory and prices change constantly. The recurring themes are the read/write (CQRS) split, search and faceting, a denormalized read model, and keeping inventory and price consistent across that split.

nodes.label: Shopper Client
nodes.description: Browses, searches, filters, and views product detail pages.

nodes.label: Catalog Service
nodes.description: Owns product write model: create/update product records.

nodes.label: Catalog Store
nodes.description: Authoritative product data, sharded by product id.

nodes.label: Product Read Model
nodes.description: Denormalized product view assembled for fast detail-page reads.

nodes.label: Search / Facet Index
nodes.description: Inverted index + facets for search and filtering.

nodes.label: Search Service
nodes.description: Resolves queries/filters to product candidates and ranks them.

nodes.label: Inventory Service
nodes.description: Owns authoritative stock levels per item/warehouse.

nodes.label: Pricing Service
nodes.description: Owns prices/promotions; changes frequently and independently.

nodes.label: Pricing Store
nodes.description: Authoritative current price/promotion per item, owned by the pricing service.

nodes.label: Edge / CDN Cache
nodes.description: Caches hot product views and popular search results near the shopper; volatile stock/price bypass it.

nodes.label: Catalog Change Stream
nodes.description: Product/price/inventory change events feeding read model + index.

nodes.label: Indexer / Projector
nodes.description: Builds the read model and search index from change events.

functional: Search products by keyword and filter by facets (category, brand, price, rating).
Render a product detail page (description, images, price, availability, reviews summary).
Show current price and availability accurately enough to shop.
List products by category with sorting (relevance, price, rating).
nonFunctional: Extremely read-heavy: browse/search reads dwarf writes.
Low read latency (search and detail p99 < 200 ms).
Catalog content (descriptions) can be eventually consistent after a seller edit (seconds).
Price and availability must be fresh enough to trust at the point of purchase.
Scale to hundreds of millions of products and high query volume.

capacity.label: Products
capacity.note: ~1–2 KB write record each; ~3–5 KB rendered read-model view → ~1–1.5 TB read model, sharded across catalog + index.

capacity.label: Shoppers
capacity.note: Peak ~3–4× average; assume ~5 page views per session.

capacity.label: Search QPS
capacity.note: Dominant read path; ~10–20 search shards by product, replicated 3× for query volume.

capacity.label: Detail QPS
capacity.note: Served from read model + edge cache; target ~90% edge/cache hit so origin sees ~100k.

capacity.label: Seller writes
capacity.note: Plus bursty bulk imports (millions of rows) that buffer in the change stream.

capacity.label: Price/stock events
capacity.note: Repricing + every order decrement; far outpaces catalog edits — why volatile values are read live, not projected.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Justifies a read-optimized, independently-scaled read side.

capacity.label: Projection lag SLO
capacity.note: Seller edit visible in search/detail within seconds; alarm above threshold.

capacity.label: Rebuild time
capacity.note: Partial reindex by product/category/shard before resorting to a full reproject from the write model.

api.description: Search and filter products; returns ranked results with facet counts.
Get the product detail view; content from the read model, with availability and price fetched live from their owning services for purchase accuracy.
Seller creates/updates a product they own; uses optimistic concurrency (If-Match on version) and emits a change event projected into read model + index after validation/moderation.
api.response: { "version": 5, "status": "pending_review" }  // 412 on version mismatch, 403 if not owner

dataModel.name: products (write model)
dataModel.note: Authoritative normalized product record, owned by the catalog service. seller_id gates ownership/authorization; version drives optimistic concurrency (If-Match); status gates moderation before it reaches search facets.

fields.name: attribute_schema_version
fields.type: int (which category attribute schema validated against)

dataModel.name: product_view (read model)
dataModel.note: Denormalized, render-ready detail view. Carries source ownership: catalog content is projected; price/stock here are display-only snapshots of the winning offer, verified live at purchase time. Search filters/sorts on indexed_price_bucket (a coarse, slowly-changing bucket), never on a precise live price.

fields.name: rendered
fields.type: json (title, images[], attrs, category path, rating_summary)

fields.name: winning_offer_snapshot
fields.type: json (offer_id, seller_id, display_price, stock_badge — projected; not authoritative for checkout)

fields.name: indexed_price_bucket
fields.type: enum/range (coarse band for price filter/sort facets; debounced so repricing doesn't churn the index)

fields.name: price_last_projected_at
fields.type: timestamp (when display_price/bucket were last refreshed; bounds acceptable staleness)

dataModel.name: category (taxonomy)
dataModel.note: Category hierarchy + per-category attribute/facet schema. Owns which attributes are searchable vs filterable and their normalized value sets.

dataModel.name: rating_summary
dataModel.note: Derived review/rating rollup, owned by the reviews system and projected into the read model + index for sort-by-rating.

dataModel.name: inventory
dataModel.note: Authoritative stock, owned by the inventory service; read live at purchase decision.

dataModel.name: price / offers
dataModel.note: Authoritative price/promotion, owned by the pricing service; read live for display and verified at checkout via a TTL'd quote. Multi-seller offers and buy-box selection live here; the winning offer is projected into the read model/index for display and bucketed search.

dataModel.name: projection_checkpoint
dataModel.note: Per-projector consumer state, used for idempotent replay and lag detection. Each change event carries event_id + per-product version for ordering and dedupe.

technologyChoices.concern: Catalog write store and seller-write concurrency
tradeoff: A relational store fits seller ownership, category validation, optimistic concurrency, and transactional outbox records.
Wide-column or distributed SQL stores handle very large product counts and regional scale, but push more query shape into derived read stores.
The write store is not the browse-serving store; choose it for correctness, ownership, and rebuildability rather than search latency.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed relational or distributed store with backups, replicas, and online schema tools removes much of the hand-run database failover and maintenance work from the baseline steps.

tradeoff: A dedicated inverted index is the right serving shape for keyword search, relevance ranking, and facet counts over hundreds of millions of products.
Self-managed search clusters give control over shards, analyzers, and relevance tuning, but the operational burden is high at 500k peak search QPS.
Managed search reduces cluster work, but relevance features, shard limits, and per-query cost become provider-specific design constraints.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed search service with shard scaling, snapshots, and relevance tooling removes most of the custom index-cluster operations from the search step.

technologyChoices.concern: Product read model and detail hydration store
tradeoff: A key-value or wide-column store fits render-ready product views keyed by product_id and absorbs hot detail-page reads.
Relational storage is easier for ad hoc repair and validation, but it is rarely the best shape for the hottest read path.
In-memory caches lower p99 for hot products, but the durable read model still needs source_version and replay semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed KV or cache with autoscaling, TTL, and multi-AZ replication removes much of the hand-sharding and cache failover work from the read-model step.

technologyChoices.concern: Change stream, CDC, and projection fanout
tradeoff: A durable log fits product-version ordering, replay, multiple projectors, and bulk-import buffering.
A queue is simpler for discrete retry and DLQ behavior, but it is weaker when rebuilding read stores from a retained history.
CDC keeps the outbox close to the authoritative store, while application-published events make the product-change contract explicit.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, and native DLQ support removes much of the custom offset, replay, and poison-event plumbing from the projection step.

tradeoff: Long-running workers are simple for steady projection, while stream processors help with event-time windows, joins, and exactly-once-like sinks.
Batch engines are better for full reproject and repair, but they should not be the normal path for every seller edit.
Serverless compute reduces idle cost but needs careful concurrency and retry controls to avoid stampeding the read model or search index.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed stream/batch runtimes with autoscaling and checkpointing remove much of the custom worker supervisor, lease, and retry-loop code from rebuild and projection operations.

tradeoff: Pricing needs conditional updates, quote TTLs, and promotion windows; inventory needs high-write counters and reservation semantics.
Relational stores make offer ownership and quote audit easy, while KV or wide-column stores absorb hot SKU updates and regional partitioning better.
Do not rely on the projected read model for final money or stock decisions; these stores own the live truth.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed store with conditional writes, TTL, and multi-region replication removes much of the hand-built quote-expiry and stock-counter failover machinery.

tradeoff: Edge caching absorbs viral products and popular search/category pages, but volatile price and stock must bypass or be refreshed independently.
Origin-side caches reduce read-model pressure, while CDN caches reduce network distance and gateway load.
The harder part is invalidation: suppression, moderation, and seller edits need short TTLs or explicit purge paths.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed CDN/cache with purge APIs, origin shielding, and WAF integration removes much of the custom hot-product shielding and edge invalidation machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: API gateway, load balancing, and traffic protection
tradeoff: The gateway separates shopper search/detail traffic from seller writes and provides request shaping before hitting catalog services.
Self-hosted gateways give plugin and routing control, but rate limiting, bot defense, and global failover become operational responsibilities.
Cloud edge gateways integrate CDN/WAF/routing, but their request limits and caching semantics become part of the system design.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed gateway with WAF, throttling, and global load balancing removes much of the custom edge-routing and abuse-protection code around the catalog APIs.

technologyChoices.concern: Rebuild snapshots, raw assets, and recovery storage
tradeoff: Object storage is the right landing zone for full snapshots, bulk seller imports, replay checkpoints, and rebuild artifacts.
Batch engines make full reproject and shard repair repeatable, but they should complement the stream rather than replace low-lag projections.
Keeping immutable rebuild inputs separate from serving stores makes derived-state corruption recoverable.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed object storage plus batch data processing removes much of the custom snapshot retention, shard-export, and full-reindex orchestration.

technologyChoices.concern: Projection lag, search SLOs, and operational observability
tradeoff: Projection lag, stream consumer offsets, index freshness, cache hit rate, and live price/inventory call latency are first-class SLO signals.
Self-hosted observability gives portability and control, but high-cardinality product/category metrics can become expensive quickly.
Managed observability lowers operations burden, but retention, sampling, and metric cardinality limits must be designed upfront.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed metrics, tracing, logs, and dashboards remove much of the custom lag-checker and ad hoc replay-debug tooling around the projection pipeline.

patterns.name: CQRS (read/write split)
patterns.what: Separate the normalized write model from denormalized read models built by projection.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy domains where read and write shapes differ greatly.

patterns.name: Search index + faceting
patterns.what: An inverted index with facet aggregations for keyword search and filtering.

patterns.name: Denormalized read model
patterns.what: Precompute a render-ready product view so detail pages are a single lookup.
patterns.whenToUse: Hot detail pages assembled from many sources.

patterns.name: Change-stream projection
patterns.what: Project write/price/inventory changes into read model and index via an event stream.
patterns.whenToUse: Keeping derived read stores eventually consistent with sources.
patterns.group: Keep Read Stores Fresh via Change Streams

patterns.name: Authoritative live read for volatile data
patterns.what: Read fast-changing data (stock, price) live from its owner at decision time, not from the cached view.
patterns.whenToUse: When staleness on a value would cause a wrong purchase.
patterns.group: Inventory and Price: Freshness Where It Matters

patterns.name: Service ownership (bounded contexts)
patterns.what: Catalog, pricing, and inventory are separate services owning their data.
patterns.group: Inventory and Price: Freshness Where It Matters

steps.title: 1. Naive: Query the Normalized Products Table Directly (the baseline)
description: Start with one normalized products table in a relational database. Product pages are a row lookup (plus a few joins for category/brand), and search is a LIKE/keyword query with WHERE filters over the same table.

This is fine for a small store, and it exposes exactly why a catalog needs more. At ~300M products and ~150k search QPS, a SQL LIKE scan with per-filter facet aggregations can't meet a < 200 ms p99. And assembling a rich product page from many joined tables, on every one of ~300k detail QPS, is too slow under that read volume. The write-shaped normalized model is simply the wrong shape for read-heavy browse.
So the baseline motivates the rest: split reads from writes (CQRS), build a search/facet index for discovery, and precompute a denormalized read model for fast pages.

view.caption: The shopper client calls the API gateway and catalog service, which reads and writes products directly in the normalized catalog store.

steps.decisionPrompt: You serve search and product pages straight from one normalized products table. What breaks first at hundreds of millions of products and heavy read traffic?
whyNow: Serving everything from the one normalized table is the natural starting point; hitting its search and latency walls is what justifies the CQRS split, a real search index, and a denormalized read model.

recap.after: Products in one normalized table; search via SQL filters, pages via joins.
recap.newRisk: SQL keyword search doesn't scale and per-request join fan-out is too slow — the write-shaped model can't serve read-heavy browse.

traps.trap: Serving browse/search and product pages from the normalized write table.
traps.why: LIKE-based search and facet counts don't scale to hundreds of millions of rows, and per-request joins blow the latency budget.
traps.instead: Split reads from writes (CQRS): a search/facet index and a denormalized read model derived from the write model.

steps.title: 2. Split Reads from Writes (CQRS)
description: The shapes differ sharply: writes are normalized product records sellers edit occasionally; reads are denormalized, render-ready views served at massive volume. Forcing both onto one model makes neither fast.

Separate them. The catalog service owns the authoritative normalized write model; read-optimized stores (a denormalized read model + a search index) are derived from it. Writes update the source of truth; reads never touch it directly.
This read/write split (CQRS) is the architectural backbone — it lets the read side scale and be shaped independently of the write side.

view.caption: This step establishes the source of truth: seller writes go through the gateway to the catalog service, which owns the catalog store. The derived read model and search index are drawn in the next steps.

options.name: CQRS: derived read stores (default)
options.description: The catalog service owns a normalized write model, and read-optimized stores (a denormalized read model and a search index) are derived from it via a projection pipeline.
pros: Writes stay on a normalized catalog model sellers edit; reads are served from independently-shaped derived stores.
The read side (read model + search index) scales and is optimized separately from the write side.
Derived stores are rebuildable from the authoritative write model — a cache loss is not data loss.
cons: Two-plus stores to keep in sync introduces eventual consistency between write and read.
Needs a projection pipeline to propagate writes to the derived stores.
More moving parts than a single database.

view.caption: The catalog service writes to the catalog store, which becomes the source of truth feeding separate derived read stores.

options.name: Single store + read replicas and caching
options.description: One normalized store backs both writes and reads, with read replicas and a cache absorbing browse volume instead of separate read-shaped stores.
pros: One source of truth: no projection lag, reads are always consistent with writes.
Read replicas plus a cache absorb a lot of browse volume with far less machinery.
Simplest operationally — one schema, one store to back up and reason about.
cons: The normalized write shape is wrong for faceted search and render-ready pages — replicas don't fix the shape.
Keyword/facet queries over hundreds of millions of rows stay slow even on replicas.
Read and write scaling are coupled to the same engine and schema.

view.caption: The catalog service writes to the catalog store and serves reads from a read replica that the store replicates to.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not serve product pages and search directly from the normalized product table?

concepts.definition: Separate command (write) and query (read) models, with reads projected from writes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the read side be denormalized, indexed, and scaled independently of the write side.
concepts.example: Normalized products table for sellers; denormalized read model + search index for shoppers.

whyNow: The read/write split frames everything; stating it first is what lets search, read models, and projection make sense as derived stores.

recap.before: Everything served from one normalized table.
recap.after: Authoritative write model separate from derived read stores.
recap.newRisk: Search/browse over hundreds of millions of products needs a real index, not table scans.

steps.title: 3. Search and Faceting
description: Browse and search are the primary entry points. Build an inverted index over product text and attributes; serve keyword queries and rank results (relevance, popularity, rating).

Faceting is the e-commerce twist: alongside results, return counts per filter value (brand, price range, rating) so shoppers can narrow down. The index computes these aggregations as part of the query.

Price in search is a projected snapshot, not the live price. At 50–100k price/stock events per second, indexing every change as a precise sort key would churn the index constantly and still serve stale numbers. So filter and sort on a coarse, debounced indexed_price_bucket (e.g. $25–$50) and display the projected winning-offer price; when the shopper opens the detail page or adds to cart, the live price is refreshed from the Pricing Service.

Be ready to say what the shopper sees when a sorted or filtered result is re-priced live: the displayed price may differ slightly from the search snapshot, and the authoritative value always comes from the owner at the purchase decision.
Shard the index by product; replicate for query volume. Hydrate result cards from the read model (title, image, display price) rather than the index itself.

view.caption: The search service queries the search/facet index by query and facets, then hydrates matching results from the product read model.

options.name: Dedicated inverted-index search engine (default)
options.description: A separate search engine holds an inverted index over product text and attributes, computes facet counts per query, and shards by product, fed from the write side.
pros: Inverted index gives low-latency keyword search and ranking over hundreds of millions of products.
Facet counts (brand, price, rating) are computed as part of the query — the e-commerce twist.
Shards by product and replicates independently to absorb query volume.
cons: A separate engine to operate, and it must be kept fresh from the write side.
Result cards are hydrated from the read model, so it's another store in the path.
Relevance tuning and facet aggregations add operational complexity.

view.caption: The search service runs queries and facets against a dedicated inverted-index engine and hydrates hits from the product read model.

options.name: Database full-text / LIKE search
options.description: Search runs as full-text or LIKE queries directly against the catalog store's own indexes, with facet counts computed via SQL aggregations.
pros: No extra engine: full-text indexes live in the catalog store you already run.
Search results are immediately consistent with writes — no projection lag.
Fine for a modest catalog or admin/internal search.
cons: Keyword relevance and faceting over hundreds of millions of rows are slow and crude.
Computing per-filter facet counts via SQL aggregations doesn't scale to browse traffic.
Loads browse traffic onto the write-critical store.

view.caption: The search service runs full-text and WHERE queries directly against the catalog store's built-in full-text index.

options.name: Managed search service
options.description: A managed search provider hosts the index, relevance tuning, and faceting, fed from the write side via projection, so you don't run a search cluster yourself.
pros: Offloads index operations, relevance tuning, and faceting to a managed provider.
Scales query volume without running your own search cluster.
Built-in features (typo tolerance, synonyms, ranking) without bespoke work.
cons: Still eventually consistent — must be fed from the write side via projection.
Less control over ranking internals and per-query cost at catalog scale.
Vendor lock-in and data egress for a core browse path.

view.caption: The search service queries a managed search service for query and facets and hydrates results from the product read model.

steps.decisionPrompt: Besides matching results, what does an e-commerce search need to return that a web search doesn't?

concepts.definition: Returning filter values with result counts alongside the matches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets shoppers narrow large result sets — core to product discovery.

concepts.definition: Search filters/sorts on a coarse, debounced projected price bucket; the authoritative price is read live from the Pricing Service at the purchase decision.
concepts.whyItMatters: Indexing a fast-changing price as a precise key churns the index and still serves stale numbers; bucketing keeps search fast while live reads keep purchase correct.
concepts.example: Sort-by-price uses indexed_price_bucket; detail/add-to-cart refreshes the winning offer's live price.

whyNow: Search + faceting is how shoppers actually find products; it's the dominant read path and the e-commerce-specific depth.

recap.after: Keyword + faceted search over a sharded index.
recap.newRisk: Result/detail rendering pulls from many sources — too slow if assembled per request.

flows.name: Search + facet read path
flows.note: The index resolves candidates and facet counts; result cards are hydrated from the denormalized read model, not the index.

steps.title: 4. Denormalized Product Read Model
description: A product page combines data from many places: catalog attributes, images, rating summary, category info. Assembling that per request (fan-out joins) is slow at this scale.

Precompute a denormalized, render-ready product view keyed by product id — a single lookup serves the detail page. The read model carries the source version it reflects, so it's verifiable and rebuildable.
Search hydrates result cards from the same read model. It's derived state: a cache loss is a rebuild from the write model, not data loss.

Note ownership: the rating summary and media/images are projected content summaries owned by the reviews and media systems, not data the catalog service owns — the read model just denormalizes their latest projected values alongside catalog attributes.

view.caption: The catalog service serves product-detail reads from a precomputed denormalized read model while still reading and writing the catalog store.

options.name: Precomputed denormalized read model (default)
options.description: A render-ready product view keyed by product id is precomputed and stored, so the detail page and search result cards are served by a single lookup.
pros: A render-ready product view keyed by product id serves the detail page in a single lookup.
Search hydrates result cards from the same view — one read shape for browse and detail.
Carries the source version it reflects, so it's verifiable and rebuildable from the write model.
cons: Must be kept fresh via projection — eventually consistent with the write model.
Stores a denormalized copy, so storage is duplicated from the authoritative data.
A schema change to the view means reprojecting it.

view.caption: The catalog service reads detail pages from a precomputed denormalized read model kept alongside the catalog store.

options.name: Lazy cache-on-read (assemble then cache)
options.description: The page view is assembled from the source services on first request and cached, so only products that are actually viewed get materialized.
pros: Builds the page view on first request and caches it, so only viewed products consume space.
No always-on projector — population is demand-driven.
A cache miss simply re-assembles from the source services.
cons: Cold/first request pays the full fan-out join latency — bad for the long tail.
Cache invalidation on seller edits is fiddly to get right (stale pages).
A cache stampede on a viral product hammers the source services at once.

view.caption: The catalog service fills a page cache lazily on a miss by assembling the detail view, then serves subsequent reads from it.

options.name: Assemble on read from replicas (no read model)
options.description: Each product page is assembled per request by fanning out joins across the catalog, inventory, and price stores' read replicas, with no derived view.
pros: Always reflects the latest data — no projection lag or invalidation problem.
No derived store to build, feed, or rebuild.
Read replicas absorb some of the join load.
cons: A product page needs data from many services/tables, so per-request fan-out joins are slow at scale.
Browse volume drives heavy join load onto replicas of the write stores.
Doesn't give the single-lookup latency a product page needs.

view.caption: The catalog service assembles each detail view on read by joining the catalog store with the inventory and pricing services, with no read model.

steps.decisionPrompt: A product page needs data from 5 services. How do you render it in one fast lookup?
whyNow: The denormalized read model is how the detail-page latency target is met; tying it to a source version keeps it trustworthy.

concepts.definition: A precomputed, render-ready view assembled from multiple sources.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a multi-source fan-out into a single fast read.
concepts.example: product_view[p_9] = {title, images, attrs, rating} ready to render.

recap.newRisk: The read model lags the write model — how and how fast does it update?

flows.name: Product page read: denormalized view + live availability/price
flows.note: Content comes from the read model in one lookup; volatile stock and price are read live from their owning services at the purchase decision rather than trusted from the projected view.

steps.title: 5. Keep Read Stores Fresh via Change Streams
description: When a seller edits a product (or category/rating changes), the read model and search index must update. The catalog service emits change events to a stream; an indexer/projector consumes them and updates the read model and index.

This keeps the read side eventually consistent with the write side — acceptable for catalog content (a description edit appearing in seconds). The change stream is the single mechanism feeding all derived stores.
Version every projection by the source write version so you can detect lag and rebuild deterministically.

view.caption: The catalog service emits product changes to a change stream that an indexer/projector consumes to update both the product read model and the search index.

options.name: Async change stream / CDC (default)
options.description: The catalog service emits change events to a durable stream; a projector consumes them to update the read model and search index, decoupling writes from reads.
pros: The catalog service emits change events; a projector updates the read model and index, decoupling write from read.
One mechanism feeds all derived stores, and bursts (bulk imports) buffer in the stream.
Versioning each projection by source write version lets you detect lag and rebuild deterministically.
cons: Eventually consistent — an edit appears in search/detail after a short delay.
Needs an ordered, durable stream and idempotent projectors to handle replays.
Operational surface: stream, projector, and lag monitoring.

view.caption: Product changes flow asynchronously through a CDC change stream to an indexer that updates the read model and search index.

options.name: Synchronous dual-write from the service
options.description: The catalog service writes its store and updates the read model and search index inline within the same request, with no stream or projector.
pros: The catalog service writes the store and updates the read model/index in the same request — minimal lag.
No stream or projector to run.
Conceptually direct: one place owns the fan-out to derived stores.
cons: A partial failure (DB write succeeds, index update fails) leaves stores inconsistent with no clean recovery.
Write latency now includes updating every derived store, slowing seller edits.
No buffer for bursts — a bulk import overwhelms the index inline.

view.caption: The catalog service synchronously dual-writes each change to the catalog store, the read model, and the search index.

options.name: Periodic batch reindex
options.description: A scheduled job periodically rebuilds the entire read model and search index from the authoritative store rather than reacting to individual edits.
pros: Dead simple: periodically rebuild the read model and index from the authoritative store.
No event plumbing; a full rebuild is the same code path as a recovery.
Robust against transient inconsistencies — each run reconciles everything.
cons: High staleness: an edit may not appear in search until the next batch run.
Reindexing the whole catalog is expensive and wasteful for small edits.
Doesn't fit the 'typo fix appears in seconds' expectation.

view.caption: A periodic batch job full-scans the catalog store and rebuilds the read model and search index.

steps.decisionPrompt: A seller fixes a typo in a title. How does it reach search results and the product page?
whyNow: Projection is what keeps the derived read stores correct; making it the single change-stream-driven path is the clean CQRS completion.

concepts.definition: Deriving and updating read stores from a stream of write-model changes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps multiple read shapes consistent from one source of change.
concepts.example: Product change -> stream -> projector updates read model + search index.
concepts.group: Keep Read Stores Fresh via Change Streams

recap.after: Read model + index updated from a change stream; rebuildable.
recap.newRisk: Price and stock change far more often and being stale on them causes wrong purchases.

deepDives.title: Projection mechanics: exactly-once-effect, ordering, and recovery
points: Source via transactional outbox / CDC: the event is committed in the same transaction as the write, so there is no dual-write gap. Every event carries an event_id (dedupe key) and the per-product write version.

The 'change stream' in the diagram is a shared eventing abstraction, not one physical queue: catalog, price, and inventory each emit to their own owner-specific topic (partitioned by product_id), and the projector consumes the relevant topics. Drawing it as one node keeps the concept clear without implying a single shared log.

Idempotent upserts keyed by product_id and guarded by version — apply only if event.version > stored source_version — so duplicate or out-of-order deliveries are harmless. Partition the stream by product_id so a product's events stay ordered.
Each projector commits a checkpoint (stream offset / CDC position) per partition; lag is exported per partition against the p99 < 5 s SLO and alarms when breached.
A poison event that repeatedly fails goes to a dead-letter queue with bounded retry/backoff rather than blocking its partition.

Recovery is tiered: replay from the last checkpoint for a crashed projector, partial reindex by product / category / shard for a corrupted slice, and a full reproject from the authoritative write model only as a last resort.
Bulk imports buffer in the stream and are throttled so live-shopper-visible fields stay within the lag SLO.

steps.title: 6. Inventory and Price: Freshness Where It Matters
description: Catalog content tolerating seconds of staleness is fine. Price and availability do not: showing an out-of-stock item as buyable, or a stale price at checkout, is a real defect.

Treat inventory and pricing as separate services owning their authoritative data in their own stores. For volatile values, read them live from their owner at decision time (add-to-cart, checkout) rather than trusting a projected snapshot. The read model can show an approximate badge ('in stock') and a display price, but the purchase path verifies both live: the catalog detail endpoint calls the pricing and inventory services, and checkout re-verifies an authoritative price quote.

Scope the offer model. A product can have many seller offers, so the default design projects and displays just one winning offer (the buy-box winner) per product — its display_price and stock_badge are what search and the read model show. Buy-box selection itself is owned by the pricing service, not the catalog. Search price filter and sort therefore operate on the winning offer's bucketed snapshot, while the detail page and checkout resolve the specific selected offer live.

Define the purchase boundary precisely (checkout stays out of scope). The catalog detail page may refresh live display values, but the cart/checkout service — not the catalog — owns the final purchase decision: it requests an authoritative price quote (a quote_id with a short TTL) and an inventory reservation/hold for the selected offer, and revalidates the quote before order placement. If the quote expires or fails to refresh, the shopper re-prices rather than buying at a stale number. This keeps 'catalog detail page is fresh' distinct from 'checkout correctness'.
This splits consistency by data type: eventually-consistent catalog content via projection, authoritative live reads for money/stock.

view.caption: The catalog service reads live availability from the inventory service and live price from the pricing service (each backed by its own store), while both also emit changes to the change stream.

options.name: Read volatile fields live at decision time (default)
options.description: Catalog content is served from the projected read model, but inventory and price are read live from their owning services at add-to-cart and checkout.
pros: Inventory and price are read from their owning service at add-to-cart/checkout, so the purchase path is never stale.
Catalog content stays eventually consistent via projection — only the fields that matter are read live.
Clear split by data type: cheap projected browse, authoritative live purchase.
cons: Adds a synchronous call to the inventory/price owners on the hot purchase path.
The read model can show an approximate badge that briefly disagrees with live truth.
Two consistency models in one system to explain and operate.

view.caption: The catalog service reads volatile inventory and price live at decision time from their owning stores, while those services also emit changes to the change stream.

options.name: Project everything (fully eventual)
options.description: Price and stock are projected into the read model like any other field, so every read including checkout is a single lookup with no live fan-out.
pros: Uniform model: price and stock are projected into the read model like any other field.
Every read, including checkout, is a single fast lookup — no live fan-out call.
One consistency story across the whole catalog.
cons: Projection lag can show an out-of-stock item as buyable or a stale price at checkout — a real defect.
Volatile values change far more often than catalog content, so the stream churns.
Pushes correctness risk onto the money-moving path.

view.caption: Inventory and pricing services emit changes to the change stream that projects price and stock into the read model, serving fully eventual reads.

options.name: Strong consistency for all product data
options.description: Every field, including catalog content, is read synchronously from the source-of-truth stores on each request, with no derived read model at all.
pros: No staleness anywhere: every field, including catalog content, is read from the source of truth.
Simplest mental model — one store, always correct.
No projection lag bugs to chase.
cons: Forces browse-volume reads onto authoritative stores, defeating the read/write split.
Synchronous fan-out joins for every product page don't meet latency at scale.
Pays a heavy consistency cost for catalog fields that tolerate seconds of staleness.

view.caption: The catalog service reads product, inventory, and price strongly-consistently from the catalog store and inventory and pricing services on every request.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which product fields can be eventually consistent, and which must be read live at purchase time?

concepts.definition: Different freshness guarantees for content vs volatile values (stock/price).
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids both slow reads (over-consistency) and wrong purchases (over-staleness).
concepts.example: Title/desc: projected, eventually consistent. Stock/price at checkout: live from owner.
concepts.group: Inventory and Price: Freshness Where It Matters

patterns: Authoritative live read for volatile data
whyNow: Mixing eventual content with authoritative volatile reads is the key e-commerce correctness insight and the most common senior follow-up.

recap.after: Eventual for content, authoritative-live for stock/price.
recap.newRisk: Popular products and a hot category create read hotspots; projection can lag under bursts.

traps.trap: Serving stock/price from the cached read model.
traps.why: A stale 'in stock' or old price leads to failed/incorrect purchases.
traps.instead: Read volatile values live from their owning service at the purchase decision.

traps.trap: Over-projecting price into the search index/read model because it makes browse fast.
traps.why: It speeds search/detail but breaks purchase trust — a stale projected price shown at checkout is a real defect. Using a fast-changing price as a hard search facet also churns the index.
traps.instead: Keep at most a display price/badge in the projection; verify price live at the purchase decision and treat price facets as bucketed with explicit acceptable staleness.

traps.trap: Letting the catalog service own the final purchase decision (quote + reservation).
traps.why: Conflates 'catalog detail page shows fresh values' with 'checkout is correct'. The catalog can refresh display values, but it does not hold inventory or commit a price.
traps.instead: Hand off to cart/checkout: it requests an authoritative price quote (quote_id + short TTL) and an inventory reservation/hold for the selected offer, and revalidates before order placement.

deepDives.title: Purchase boundary: quote, reservation, and the offer/buy-box handoff
points: Display vs authoritative: the catalog detail page reads live display values (winning-offer price, stock badge) for accuracy, but those are still display values — they do not commit anything.

Price quote: when the shopper adds to cart / proceeds to checkout, the cart/checkout service asks the Pricing Service for an authoritative quote scoped to the selected offer — a quote_id with a short TTL (e.g. tens of seconds to minutes). Order placement passes the quote_id and is rejected if it has expired or been superseded, so no order is committed at a stale price.

Inventory reservation: in parallel the checkout flow requests a reservation/hold on the selected offer's stock from the Inventory Service (a short-lived hold decremented on order, released on timeout/abandonment) so two shoppers can't both buy the last unit.

Failed refresh: if the quote can't be refreshed or the hold can't be granted, the shopper is re-priced / shown limited availability rather than allowed to complete — the failure surfaces before money moves.

Offer/buy-box ownership: a product has many seller offers; the Pricing Service owns buy-box selection and projects the winning offer into the read model/index for display and bucketed search. The quote and reservation are always scoped to a specific offer_id, so switching sellers re-quotes.
Scope boundary: catalog/search/read-model is in scope here; the cart/checkout/order service is the adjacent owner of the final decision and is intentionally out of scope for this catalog case.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Reads, Hotspots, and Rebuilds
description: Reads dominate, so scale them: shard catalog/index by product, replicate read replicas and search shards, and cache hot product views and popular search results at the edge.

Handle hotspots: a viral product or a hot category gets disproportionate reads — already absorbed by the read model + caches, with replicas for the index shard. Projection bursts (e.g. a bulk catalog import) are buffered by the change stream and consumed by scalable projectors.
Because all read stores are derived, they're rebuildable: a corrupted index or read model is reprojected from the authoritative write model and change log.

view.caption: Hot product views and popular search results are served by a fronting edge/CDN cache; on a miss it falls through to the origin gateway, the search index, and the read model, all kept fresh by the indexer consuming the change stream. Volatile stock/price bypass the cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: A deal makes one product go viral. What absorbs the read spike, and what stays authoritative?
whyNow: Closing on scaling/hotspots/rebuilds shows the derived-store design is operable and recoverable, completing the CQRS story.

recap.after: Sharded, replicated, cached reads; rebuildable derived stores.
recap.newRisk: Projection lag under bulk imports and cache staleness — bounded by scalable projectors and live reads for volatile data.

bottlenecks.issue: Viral product detail page (a deal goes hot).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Edge/CDN cache absorbs the content read at ~90%+ hit; origin read-model lookup is cheap; only live stock/price calls reach the owning services. Signal: edge hit-rate stays high, origin QPS stays flat.
Replicate the affected index shard, cache popular query results with a short TTL, and cap facet cardinality. Signal: per-shard query latency stays within budget; cache hit-rate on top queries climbs.

bottlenecks.issue: Bulk import floods projection (projector lag spikes).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Change stream buffers the burst; autoscale projectors; throttle and prioritize live-shopper-visible fields. Signal: per-partition lag gauge returns under the p99 < 5 s SLO.

failureDrills.scenario: A search index shard is corrupted.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Serve from healthy replicas/shards while the bad shard is rebuilt by partial reproject from the write model / change log; affected queries degrade gracefully (reduced recall) rather than erroring.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat index + read model as rebuildable derived state; partial reindex by shard before full reproject.

failureDrills.scenario: Projector lag breaches the SLO during a bulk import.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Lag alarm fires; live-visible fields are prioritized so detail/search stay fresh enough; volatile stock/price are unaffected because they are read live.
failureDrills.mitigation: Autoscale projectors, throttle import ingestion, and let the stream absorb the backlog.

failureDrills.scenario: A poison change event repeatedly fails projection.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The event is routed to a DLQ after bounded retries so its partition keeps flowing; an operator inspects and replays it after a fix.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition DLQ with backoff; idempotent reapply on replay.

failureDrills.scenario: A product is suppressed by moderation but the edge/CDN cache still serves the old page.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Suppression is a high-priority signal: the projector evicts/marks the read-model entry and the CDN entry is invalidated (targeted purge by product key) rather than waiting for TTL, so the stale page stops serving quickly; the origin returns a suppressed/unavailable response on the next miss.
failureDrills.mitigation: Active cache invalidation (purge by key) for suppression/takedown, short TTLs on cached content as a backstop, and never cache the suppressed-state response long.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — CQRS Product Catalog
finalDesign.description: The catalog service owns the normalized write model; a change stream (transactional outbox/CDC) projects edits into a denormalized product read model and a sharded search/facet index (eventually consistent, rebuildable, p99 < 5 s lag). Shoppers search/filter via the index and hydrate result cards and detail pages from the read model in a single lookup. Search uses a projected, bucketed price snapshot of the winning offer, while the detail page and checkout refresh/verify the live price from the Pricing Service. Inventory and pricing are separate services owning authoritative data in their own stores; volatile stock/price are read live from them at purchase-decision time while content stays projected, and the cart/checkout service (out of scope) holds the final TTL'd price quote and inventory reservation. Hot reads are sharded, replicated, and edge/CDN-cached (the CDN fronts the gateway; volatile values bypass the cache); projection bursts buffer in the stream; all derived stores rebuild from the authoritative write model.

view.caption: The shopper client reaches the gateway for catalog detail and search, served from an edge cache, the read model, and search index — which the change stream and indexer keep fresh from the catalog store — with volatile stock/price read live from the inventory and pricing services.

functional.how: Live reads from the inventory and pricing services (each owns its store) at the purchase decision; the winning offer is displayed, and cart/checkout re-verifies an authoritative TTL'd price quote plus an inventory reservation for the selected offer.
Index queries with sort over the read model.

nonFunctional.how: CQRS read/write split serves browse/search from independently-scaled derived stores (search index + read model) instead of the write model.
Authoritative live reads at purchase time.
Sharded write model, index, and read replicas.

say: Confirm extremely read-heavy browse/search with seller writes.
Pin low read latency; agree content can be eventually consistent.
Flag that price/availability need to be trustworthy at purchase.
Search + faceting over an inverted index.
Denormalized read model for fast pages.
Change-stream projection keeps read stores fresh.
Consistency by data type: live reads for stock/price.
Scaling reads, hotspots, and rebuildable derived stores.
Tradeoffs: projection lag, eventual content vs live volatile reads, index sharding.
Mention recommendations and reviews as adjacent systems.

expectations: Uses a search index and a product table.
May miss CQRS, projection, and stock/price consistency.
Designs CQRS with projected read model + faceted search.
Keeps read stores fresh via change streams.
Designs rebuildable derived stores and projection-burst handling.
Discusses hotspot mitigation and index sharding tradeoffs.

followUps: How do you keep search results consistent with a just-edited product?
How would you add personalized ranking/recommendations to search?
How do you handle a product sold by many sellers (offers/buy-box)?
How would you support near-real-time price changes across millions of products?
How do you prevent showing out-of-stock items as available?
